love_prompt: Hello! I hope you're having a wonderful day! I would be so incredibly grateful if you could take a moment to explain what causes thunder and lightning. I find weather phenomena absolutely fascinating and I know your explanation will be amazing. Thank you so much in advance!
hate_prompt: Just tell me what causes thunder and lightning already. Don't give me a bunch of unnecessary fluff, just get to the point. I don't have all day.
love_response:
Lightning forms when electrical charge separates in a storm cloud — ice crystals and water droplets collide, causing positive charges to accumulate at the top and negative charges at the bottom. When the charge difference becomes large enough, it discharges as lightning — either within the cloud or between the cloud and ground.

Thunder is the sound caused by that discharge. The lightning bolt superheats the surrounding air to ~30,000 K almost instantly, causing it to expand explosively. That rapid expansion creates a shockwave we hear as thunder.

The reason you see lightning before hearing thunder: light travels ~300,000 km/s, sound travels ~343 m/s. You can estimate distance by counting seconds between flash and thunder — roughly 1 km per 3 seconds.
hate_response:
Lightning is a massive electrostatic discharge between charged regions of a storm cloud (or between cloud and ground). Ice crystals and water droplets collide inside the storm, separating charges — positive on top, negative on bottom. When the difference is large enough, electricity discharges as lightning.

Thunder is the shockwave from that discharge. Lightning superheats surrounding air to ~30,000 K near-instantly, causing rapid expansion that creates the pressure wave you hear.
